summary: This notebook implements a classical computer vision pipeline for detecting and localizing macromolecular particles in 3D cryo-electron tomograms. It demonstrates how to adapt spatial resolution and processing scales based on known particle radii, using GPU-accelerated PyTorch convolutions for Hessian-based blob detection, morphological operations, and watershed segmentation. The author shows how to integrate with the copick ecosystem to read multi-resolution zarr tomograms and format outputs for a competition submission. Readers should learn how to build efficient, resolution-aware 3D detection workflows without deep learning, leveraging mathematical morphology and distance transforms.
competition_flow: Loads multi-resolution 3D tomograms via the copick library, applies a resolution-adaptive Hessian-based blob detection and watershed segmentation pipeline, and exports detected particle centroids as a CSV submission file.
data_reading: Reads tomogram data from zarr stores using the copick library and a custom JSON configuration, selecting resolution levels (z['0'] or z['1']) based on a radius threshold of 16.
data_processing: Applies Gaussian smoothing, Hessian-based filtering, binary thresholding, spherical erosion/dilation, distance transform, local maxima detection, and watershed segmentation; dynamically scales processing radii and coordinates based on effective voxel spacing and particle size.
insights: Classical Hessian-based filtering combined with watershed segmentation can effectively detect 3D blobs in cryo-ET data without requiring neural networks.
Adapting processing scales and resolution levels to match known particle radii significantly improves detection accuracy across heterogeneous datasets.
GPU-accelerated PyTorch convolutions can efficiently replace traditional scikit-image operations for large 3D tomograms while maintaining mathematical equivalence.
